member.name: Gareth Snell
member.synopsis: Gareth Snell is the Labour (Co-op) MP for Stoke-on-Trent Central , and has been an MP continually since 4 July 2024.

officialSourceNotes: Current member identity, party, constituency, thumbnail, contact information, synopsis, written questions, and registered interests are generated from the official UK Parliament Members API.
Recent Commons division positions are generated from the official UK Parliament Commons Votes API.
External official links are retained as citations; TOR Phi profile and record navigation remains internal.

writtenQuestions.heading: Arts: Public Appointments
writtenQuestions.questionText: To ask the Secretary of State for Culture, Media and Sport, whether representatives of the visual arts sector will be included in the membership of the working group on independent and smaller creative organisations.
writtenQuestions.answerText: We are in the process of establishing the working group, and have reached out to prospective members including independent creatives, creative industries trade and member organisations, and individuals with expertise in law and licensing. We are now confi...
writtenQuestions.answeringBody: Department for Culture, Media and Sport

writtenQuestions.heading: Artificial Intelligence: Copyright
writtenQuestions.questionText: To ask the Secretary of State for Culture, Media and Sport, whether the proposed working group on independent and smaller creative organisations will consider measures to prevent the unauthorised use of copyright-protected works by AI systems.
writtenQuestions.answerText: We are in the process of establishing the working group, and have reached out to prospective members including independent creatives, creative industries trade and member organisations, and individuals with expertise in law and licensing. We are now confi...

writtenQuestions.heading: Terrorism: Northern Ireland
writtenQuestions.questionText: To ask the Secretary of State for Northern Ireland, what recent assessment he has made of trends in the level of security in Northern Ireland.
writtenQuestions.answerText: The Threat Level in Northern Ireland is under constant review by the Joint Terrorism Analysis Centre (JTAC). There is a formal assessment of the Threat Level, which is reviewed twice yearly. This was done most recently in March - where the threat level wa...
writtenQuestions.answeringBody: Northern Ireland Office

writtenQuestions.heading: World Cup: Gambling
writtenQuestions.questionText: To ask the Secretary of State for Culture, Media and Sport, whether her Department has had discussions with payment providers on reducing the flow of funds from UK consumers to illegal gambling operators ahead of the 2026 FIFA World Cup.
writtenQuestions.answerText: We are committed to taking action on the illegal gambling market. In the past year, we have established the Illegal Gambling Taskforce, which includes working with payment providers to help tackle the issue of payments relating to illegal gambling. We pla...
writtenQuestions.answeringBody: Department for Culture, Media and Sport

writtenQuestions.answerText: We are committed to taking action on the illegal gambling market. In the past year, we have established the Illegal Gambling Taskforce, which includes working with payment providers to help tackle the issue of payments relating to illegal gambling. We pla...

writtenQuestions.heading: World Cup: Gambling

writtenQuestions.answerText: We are committed to taking action on the illegal gambling market. In the past year, we have established the Illegal Gambling Taskforce, which includes working with payment providers to help tackle the issue of payments relating to illegal gambling. We pla...

writtenQuestions.heading: Horse Racing: Gambling
writtenQuestions.questionText: To ask the Secretary of State for Culture, Media and Sport, what estimate she has made of a) number of bets placed with and b) total value of stakes wagered with illegal gambling websites and operators during the Royal Ascot festival.
writtenQuestions.answerText: The Government does not hold this information. We are taking action on the illegal gambling market, and as part of the 2025 Budget, HMT announced £26 million had been allocated to the Gambling Commission to increase investment, resources and capacity to f...
writtenQuestions.answeringBody: Department for Culture, Media and Sport

writtenQuestions.heading: Government Departments: Procurement
writtenQuestions.questionText: To ask the Minister for the Cabinet Office, what recent discussions he has Cabinet colleagues on increasing levels of insourcing.
writtenQuestions.answerText: This Labour Government is committed to delivering the biggest wave of insourcing of public services in a generation, ending outsourcing by default.On 17 June, the Chief Secretary to the Prime Minister, the Chancellor and I introduced a new Public Interest...
writtenQuestions.answeringBody: Cabinet Office

writtenQuestions.heading: Slavery: Deportation
writtenQuestions.questionText: To ask the Secretary of State for the Home Department, how many victims of modern slavery with a positive conclusive grounds decision have been (a) administratively removed or deported from the UK and (b) issued a notice of removal; and for how many was t
writtenQuestions.answerText: The Home Office publishes data on returns Immigration System Statistics quarterly release(opens in a new tab) Data on returns from the UK, broken down by enforced returns (of which ‘deportations’ is a legalised subset) and Returns from the UK, by type of ...
writtenQuestions.answeringBody: Home Office

writtenQuestions.heading: Government Departments: Procurement
writtenQuestions.questionText: To ask the Minister for the Cabinet Office, when he will bring forward a Procurement Bill.
writtenQuestions.answerText: The Government will bring forward a Procurement Bill when parliamentary time allows. In March I announced non-legislative reforms to procurement underpinned by three principles. Firstly, government procurement should do much more to protect national secur...
writtenQuestions.answeringBody: Cabinet Office

writtenQuestions.heading: NHS: Contracts
writtenQuestions.questionText: To ask the Secretary of State for Health and Social Care, what assessment he has made of the services that could be insourced in the NHS.
writtenQuestions.answerText: National Health Service commissioners are responsible for commissioning the appropriate services for their population. NHS providers may choose to sub-contract activity to other providers, such as insourcing companies, as necessary, in order to ensure pat...
writtenQuestions.answeringBody: Department of Health and Social Care

writtenQuestions.heading: Local Government
writtenQuestions.questionText: To ask the Secretary of State for Housing, Communities and Local Government, what assessment he has made of the services that could be brought in-house in local authorities.
writtenQuestions.answerText: While local authorities are independent bodies responsible for their own procurement decisions, the Cabinet Office, as responsible for procurement policy, is assessing how guidance can support local authorities to identify opportunities for bringing key l...
writtenQuestions.answeringBody: Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government

writtenQuestions.heading: Recycling: Energy
writtenQuestions.questionText: To ask the Secretary of State for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs, what steps she is taking to support the circular economy sector with energy costs related to material reprocessing and recycling.
writtenQuestions.answerText: The only way to bring energy bills down sustainably is by reducing Britain’s exposure to volatile fossil fuel markets. The Government’s mission for Clean Power by 2030 will get us off the rollercoaster of fossil fuel prices, to cut bills for businesses an...
writtenQuestions.answeringBody: Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs

writtenQuestions.heading: Small Businesses: Energy
writtenQuestions.questionText: To ask the Secretary of State for Business and Trade, whether he is taking steps to encourage the development of insurance products to protect small businesses against sudden energy price spikes.
writtenQuestions.answerText: The government recognises that energy costs are a real concern for small businesses. Government supports a competitive insurance market and encourages firms to shop around for suitable cover. Businesses struggling to secure insurance can contact the Briti...
writtenQuestions.answeringBody: Department for Business and Trade

writtenQuestions.heading: Industry: Energy
writtenQuestions.questionText: To ask the Secretary of State for Business and Trade, whether he has made a comparative assessment of industrial energy subsidies (a) provided by the US under the Inflation Reduction Act and (b) available to UK firms.
writtenQuestions.answerText: The Department for Business and Trade has not made a comparative assessment of industrial energy subsidies provided by the US under the Inflation Reduction Act.The Government remains committed to ensuring British businesses are internationally competitive...
writtenQuestions.answeringBody: Department for Business and Trade

writtenQuestions.heading: Business: Energy
writtenQuestions.questionText: To ask the Secretary of State for Business and Trade, what assessment he has made of the potential impact of business energy costs on the utilisation rates of UK-EU trade preferences under the Trade and Cooperation Agreement.
writtenQuestions.answerText: We are closely monitoring the impact of rising energy prices, driven by global events. We have announced measures to help businesses most impacted by those rising energy prices to electrify their heating and provide greater certainty over energy bills as ...
writtenQuestions.answeringBody: Department for Business and Trade

writtenQuestions.heading: Business: Energy
writtenQuestions.questionText: To ask the Secretary of State for Business and Trade, whether he plans to publish key performance indicators on the impact of Government energy support on business productivity.
writtenQuestions.answerText: The Government currently does not have any plans to publish key performance indicators on the impact of current energy support schemes on business productivity. The Government has also not measured the impact of energy support schemes on the productivity ...
writtenQuestions.answeringBody: Department for Business and Trade
